name: Al-Afuw

names.en: The Pardoner, the Effacer of sins

title: Al-Afuw: The Pardoner, the One who effaces the sin
meta_description: Al-Afuw, the Pardoner: not only the One who forgives but the One who erases the sin entirely, wiping the record clean as if it never was. Built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Qurtubi), with the famous dua of Laylat al-Qadr. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Al-Afuw, Al-Afuww, Al-Afu, The Pardoner, The Effacer of sins, names of Allah, 99 names, afw, pardon vs forgiveness, Allahumma innaka afuwwun, Laylat al-Qadr dua, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, and al-Qurtubi), in the voice of Buruja.
lede: Most of us know what it is to be forgiven and still carry the thing. The other person says it is fine, they have let it go, and yet the memory of it sits there between you, a small permanent mark on the record. Forgiveness covered it. It did not erase it. And quietly we assume that is the best even God offers: that our worst moments are pardoned but never quite gone, filed away, covered over, still technically on the books.

Al-Afuw answers that assumption. This name does not only mean the One who forgives. The scholars of the language tell us afw is effacement, obliteration, the wiping away of a thing until no trace of it is left. So where Al-Ghaffar and Al-Ghafur cover the sin (their root meaning is to veil, to conceal), Al-Afuw goes further and removes it: He does not only hide the record, He erases it, as though it had never been written at all.

sections.h2: The name, and the form it is built on

ayah.arabic: ذَٰلِكَ وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِ ثُمَّ بُغِيَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنصُرَنَّهُ اللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ
ayah.text: That [is so]. And whoever responds [to injustice] with the equivalent of that with which he was harmed and then is tyrannized - Allāh will surely aid him. Indeed, Allāh is Pardoning and Forgiving.

paras: Start with the word. Al-Afuw comes from three Arabic letters, ayn, fa, waw, the root of afw. In the language, afw carries the idea of effacement: the wind effaces (taʿfu) the tracks until the path is gone, a thing is afw when it has been wiped out and left no mark. Built on this intensive form, the name names the One whose pardon is exactly that: not a softening of the penalty but an erasing of the offense.

Notice next how the Qur'an almost never lets this name stand by itself. Here in Surah Al-Hajj it arrives joined to a companion: laʿafuwwun ghafur, Pardoning and Forgiving. It comes the same way in Surah An-Nisa, kana afuwwan ghafura (4:43, 4:99), and again in Surah Al-Mujadila, laʿafuwwun ghafur (58:2). And the pairing is teaching you something. Maghfira, forgiveness, comes from a root that means to cover and conceal. Afw means to erase. Put them together and you are told that Allah both covers the sin from the eyes of creation and wipes it from the record entirely. He hides it, and then He deletes it.

Commenting on this very verse, al-Sa'di writes that Allah pardons the sinners and does not rush them to punishment, and forgives their sins, and the key phrase is what he says next: yuzeeluha, He removes them, wa yuzeelu aatharaha anhum, and He removes their very traces from them. That is the heart of this name. Not a sin covered over with the scar still showing, but a sin lifted away along with every mark it left.

sections.h2: Pardon that erases, not only forgiveness that covers
paras: It is worth slowing down on the difference, because it changes how you carry your past. Picture two ways of dealing with a stain. One is to lay a cloth over it: the stain is hidden, no one sees it, but lift the cloth and it is still there. The other is to wash it out completely, so that even you, looking for it, cannot find where it was. The first is the image inside maghfira, covering. The second is the image inside afw, effacement.

Al-Sa'di, on that same verse in Al-Hajj, calls this Allah's settled, permanent, essential description: wasfuhu al-mustaqirru al-laazimu al-dhaati, and says His dealing with His servants at all times is bil-afwi wal-maghfira, with pardon and forgiveness together. So this is not a mood that Allah is occasionally in. To pardon and to erase is who He is, the way He relates to those who turn to Him, always.

And al-Qurtubi adds the dimension that makes the mercy land with full weight. Al-afw, he writes, is an attribute of Allah alongside His full power to take retribution: min sifati Allahi taala maa al-qudrati ala al-intiqam. This is not the helpless overlooking of someone who could not have struck back anyway. This is the deliberate erasing, by the One who had every right and every ability to make you pay, and chose instead to wipe the page. We might reflect that a pardon costs the most, and means the most, precisely when it comes from the One who could have done otherwise.

_Note: the contrast between afw as effacement and maghfira as covering draws on the classical lexical sense of these roots and on al-Sa'di's gloss (He removes the sins and removes their traces); it is offered as contemplation grounded in the fetched commentary, not as a formal point of consensus among the mufassirun._

sections.h2: The mercy hidden inside a hard ruling

ayah.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَأَنتُمْ سُكَارَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا مَا تَقُولُونَ وَلَا جُنُبًا إِلَّا عَابِرِي سَبِيلٍ حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا ۚ وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ الْغَائِطِ أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا مَاءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا
ayah.text: O you who have believed, do not approach prayer while you are intoxicated until you know what you are saying or in a state of janābah, except those passing through [a place of prayer], until you have washed [your whole body]. And if you are ill or on a journey or one of you comes from the place of relieving himself or you have contacted women [i.e., had sexual intercourse] and find no water, then seek clean earth and wipe over your faces and your hands [with it]. Indeed, Allāh is ever Pardoning and Forgiving.

paras: Look at where this name is sitting. The verse is a ruling about prayer and purification, and it ends with a difficulty: what do you do when you must pray and there is no water to purify yourself? Allah answers by giving the believers tayammum, purification with clean earth, and then seals the whole verse with His name: indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and Forgiving.

Ibn Kathir draws the connection out plainly. Part of His pardoning and forgiving, he explains, is that He allows you to use tayammum and to pray after it when there is no water, in order to make things easy for you, out of His mercy and kindness for His servants. Sit with that. The name Al-Afuw is not attached here to a dramatic crime forgiven. It is attached to an ordinary practical hardship lifted. His pardon is so woven into how He deals with you that it shows up in the small mercy of letting you reach Him with dust when water is out of reach.

Al-Sa'di reads the seal of the verse the same way, that Allah is abundant in pardon and forgiveness toward His believing servants by making what He commands easy and light, so that obedience never crushes the one trying. And then he widens it: among His pardon and forgiveness, al-Sa'di says, is that He opened for the sinners the door of repentance and turning back, and called them to it, and promised them forgiveness of their sins. The same name that eases the ruling on water is the name that holds the door of return open for the one who broke far more than a rule.

sections.h2: Pardoning, and fully able to punish

ayah.arabic: إِن تُبْدُوا خَيْرًا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا عَن سُوءٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيرًا
ayah.text: If [instead] you show [some] good or conceal it or pardon an offense - indeed, Allāh is ever Pardoning and Competent.

paras: Once in the Qur'an this name is paired not with forgiveness but with power, and the pairing is deliberate. If you pardon an offense, the verse says, then know that Allah is afuwwan qadira, Pardoning and Competent, fully Able. The two are set side by side on purpose.

Ibn Kathir explains the logic: among Allah's attributes is that He pardons and forgives His servants although He is fully able to punish them. He then carries a beautiful image of the angels who bear the Throne. Some of them praise, he relates, saying all praise is due to You for Your forbearance though You have perfect knowledge of every wrong, and others praise saying all praise is due to You for Your forgiving though You have perfect ability to punish. The pardon is glorious because the power is real. Al-Qurtubi makes the same point on this verse: al-afw is Allah's attribute together with His full capacity for retribution.

This is where Al-Afuw stands apart from how we usually imagine being let off. Our pardons are often just resignation, we forgive because we cannot do anything anyway. Allah's pardon is never that. He erases what He had every right and every power to hold against you. And Ibn Kathir attaches a promise the Prophet ﷺ made to anyone who chooses this same path: no charity ever decreases wealth, and Allah only increases in honour a servant who pardons, and whoever humbles himself for Allah, Allah raises him. Pardon, in the economy of Al-Afuw, never lowers you. It lifts you.

sections.h2: The night built around this one name
paras: If you want to feel how central this name is, look at what the Prophet ﷺ taught his most beloved companion to ask for on the greatest night of the year. Aisha asked him: if I know which night is Laylat al-Qadr, the Night of Decree, what should I say? Of all the things a person could beg for on a night better than a thousand months, he gave her one short prayer, and he built it entirely on this name.

He taught her to say: Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anni. O Allah, You are Pardoning, You love to pardon, so pardon me. This is the famous dua of Laylat al-Qadr, reported by at-Tirmidhi from Aisha, and notice what it does. It does not lead with a list of requests. It approaches Allah by His name Al-Afuw, then says something striking about Him, that He loves to pardon, tuhibbu al-afwa, that erasing our sins is something He delights in, not something we have to wear Him down to obtain. And only then does it ask: so erase mine.

We might reflect on why, of every name, this is the one for that night. The point of Laylat al-Qadr is to come out of it clean, with the slate not merely covered but wiped. So the believer is taught to reach for the precise name that does that work: not only forgive me, Al-Ghafur, but efface me, Al-Afuw, leave nothing of it behind. To know this name is to know which door the Prophet ﷺ pointed his ummah toward when the stakes were highest.

sections.h2: Because He erases, He commands you to erase

ayah.text: But indeed, Allāh is Pardoning and Forgiving.

paras: A name of Allah is never only information about Him. It is a summons to become something. And Al-Afuw, more than almost any name, comes attached to a command: because He pardons you, He tells you to pardon others, and He ties His treatment of you to your treatment of them.

Al-Sa'di makes the link explicit on the verse in Al-Hajj. After naming Allah as the One whose constant dealing is pardon and forgiveness, he turns to the wronged and says: so it is fitting for you, O you who have been wronged and trespassed against, that you pardon and overlook and forgive, so that Allah will deal with you as you deal with His servants. The standard you set is the standard you invite. Hold others to the letter of their worst moment, and you ask to be held to yours. Erase theirs, and you ask the Eraser to erase yours.

Al-Qurtubi carries a report that should stop any of us mid-grudge. On the Day of Resurrection, when the nations are gathered before the Lord of the worlds, a caller will announce: let whoever has his reward due upon Allah Himself stand. And no one will rise, al-Hasan said, except those who pardoned people in the worldly life. Al-Qurtubi anchors it in the verse: fa-man afa wa-aslaha fa-ajruhu ala Allah, whoever pardons and makes peace, his reward is upon Allah (42:40). The person you find hardest to forgive may be the exact price of a reward you cannot imagine, handed to you directly by Al-Afuw.

sections.h2: Live as someone whose record can be wiped
paras: So what does it actually change, to carry this name? It changes three things.

First, it changes how you hold your own past. The believer who knows Al-Afuw does not have to drag every old sin behind them forever like a chain. Al-Sa'di reminded us that part of His pardon is the open door of repentance and the promise of forgiveness to those who come back. The sins you turned away from in sincere tawba are not merely covered, kept on file with your name on them. To the One who is Al-Afuw they are effaced, their traces removed, as al-Sa'di said of this very name. You are allowed to stop carrying what He has already erased.

Second, it changes how you pray, especially when you have nothing impressive to offer. You do not approach Al-Afuw by first cleaning yourself up enough to be worth pardoning. You approach Him precisely because He loves to pardon. The Prophet ﷺ taught the shortest, highest prayer of the year to begin not with our worthiness but with His nature: You are Pardoning, You love to pardon. So bring Him the worst of it, the thing you are most ashamed of, and ask the One who delights in erasing to erase it.

Third, it changes how you treat the people who have wronged you. You cannot honestly beg Al-Afuw to wipe your record while you keep a meticulous ledger of everyone else's. The name pulls you toward release. Al-Sa'di said pardon so that Allah pardons you; the Prophet ﷺ said pardon and be raised in honour; al-Qurtubi showed that the only ones whose reward is directly upon Allah are those who let go. A heart shaped by Al-Afuw becomes an eraser in the world, not a record-keeper, because it has felt what it is to have its own page wiped clean.

Step back and let the size of this name settle. We spend so much of our lives managing the record: hiding our worst moments, bracing for them to resurface, half-believing that even God keeps a quiet account of everything we would give anything to undo. Al-Afuw is the answer the Qur'an gives to that fear, and it is not a soft reassurance, it is a name of God.

He is not only the One who covers, who lays a veil over the sin so creation does not see it, though He is that too. He is the One who erases, who lifts the sin away and removes its very traces, who had every power to make you pay and chose instead to wash the page until even you cannot find the mark. He named this as His settled, essential way of dealing with those who turn to Him. He built the prayer of the greatest night of the year around it. And He loves, actually loves, to do it.

So you do not have to live as someone permanently marked by your worst day. To the One who is Al-Afuw, the sincere returner does not walk around with a covered-over stain. The page is wiped. That is the mercy folded into this name: not merely that you are forgiven, but that you can be made as though it never was.

dua.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ إِنَّكَ عَفُوٌّ تُحِبُّ الْعَفْوَ فَاعْفُ عَنِّي
dua.translit: Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anni
dua.en: O Allah, You are Pardoning, You love to pardon, so pardon me. (The dua the Prophet ﷺ taught Aisha for Laylat al-Qadr, reported by at-Tirmidhi. This is a hadith, not a verse of the Qur'an.)

lessons.bold: Stop carrying what He has erased.
lessons.text: Afw is effacement, not just covering. Al-Sa'di says of this name that Allah removes the sins and removes their very traces. What you turned away from in sincere tawba is not on file with your name on it. You are allowed to put it down.

lessons.bold: Approach Him because He loves to pardon.
lessons.text: You do not clean yourself up enough to deserve pardon first. The Prophet ﷺ taught the highest prayer of the year to begin with His nature: You are Pardoning, You love to pardon. Bring Him the thing you are most ashamed of and ask Him to erase it.

lessons.bold: Remember the power behind the pardon.
lessons.text: Ibn Kathir and al-Qurtubi both note Allah pardons while fully able to punish (afuwwan qadira, 4:149). This is no helpless overlooking. He erases what He had every right to hold against you, which is exactly what makes it mercy.

lessons.bold: Become an eraser, not a record-keeper.
lessons.text: Al-Sa'di teaches: pardon and overlook so that Allah deals with you as you deal with His servants. You cannot honestly ask Him to wipe your page while you keep a ledger of everyone else's.

lessons.bold: Forgive for a reward only He can give.
lessons.text: Al-Qurtubi carries the report that on the Day of Judgment only those who pardoned people in this life will have their reward due directly upon Allah (42:40). The person hardest to forgive may be the price of a reward you cannot imagine.

faq.q: What does the name Al-Afuw mean?
faq.a: Al-Afuw (العفو) means The Pardoner, from the root ayn-fa-waw, whose core sense is effacement, wiping away, leaving no trace. So Al-Afuw is not only the One who forgives but the One who erases the sin entirely. Commenting on Al-Hajj 22:60, al-Sa'di explains that Allah pardons sinners, forgives their sins, removes them, and removes their very traces from them, and calls this His settled, essential way of dealing with His servants.

faq.q: What is the difference between Al-Afuw and Al-Ghaffar / Al-Ghafur?
faq.a: Their roots point in different directions. Ghafara means to cover or conceal, so Al-Ghaffar and Al-Ghafur veil the sin, hiding it from creation and from punishment. Afw means to efface or obliterate, so Al-Afuw goes a step further and removes the sin altogether. A helpful image: maghfira lays a cloth over the stain, afw washes the stain out completely. The Qur'an often pairs the two names (for example afuwwan ghafura in 4:43), telling you Allah both covers the sin and erases it. (This contrast reflects the classical lexical sense of the roots and al-Sa'di's gloss; it is reflection grounded in the commentary, not a formal point of consensus.)

faq.q: Why is Allah called both Pardoning and Powerful in 4:149?
faq.a: The verse pairs afuww with qadir (Pardoning and Competent, fully Able) on purpose. Ibn Kathir explains that among Allah's attributes is that He pardons His servants although He is fully able to punish them, and relates that the angels who bear the Throne praise Him for forgiving though He has perfect ability to punish. Al-Qurtubi says plainly that al-afw is Allah's attribute together with His full capacity for retribution. The pardon means the most precisely because the power to do otherwise is real.

faq.q: What is the dua of Al-Afuw for Laylat al-Qadr?
faq.a: When Aisha asked the Prophet ﷺ what to say if she knew it was Laylat al-Qadr, he taught her: Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anni, 'O Allah, You are Pardoning, You love to pardon, so pardon me.' This is reported by at-Tirmidhi and is a hadith, not a verse of the Qur'an. It is striking that of all requests for the greatest night of the year, the Prophet ﷺ built the prayer entirely on this name, asking not merely to be forgiven but to be effaced, with the slate wiped clean.

faq.q: How should belief in Al-Afuw change how I treat others?
faq.a: It should make you pardon. Al-Sa'di, on 22:60, says that since pardon is Allah's constant way with His servants, the one who has been wronged should pardon and overlook so that Allah will deal with him as he deals with others. Al-Qurtubi carries a report that on the Day of Resurrection only those who pardoned people in the world will have their reward due directly upon Allah (42:40). You cannot sincerely ask Al-Afuw to wipe your record while keeping a careful ledger of everyone else's.

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We spend so much of our lives managing a record: hiding our worst moments, bracing for them to come back, half-believing that even God keeps a quiet account of everything we would give anything to undo. Al-Afuw is the answer the Qur'an gives to that fear, and it is not a soft reassurance, it is a name of God. He is not only the One who covers the sin, He is the One who erases it, who lifts it away and removes its very traces, as al-Sa'di said of this name, who had every power to make us pay and chose instead to wash the page clean. He named this as His settled, essential way with those who turn to Him, He built the prayer of the greatest night of the year around it, and He loves to do it. So you do not have to live permanently marked by your worst day. To the One who is Al-Afuw, the sincere returner does not carry a covered-over stain. The page is wiped, as though it never was.

O Allah, Al-Afuw, the Pardoner, You do not only cover our sins, You erase them and remove their every trace, and You do it while fully able to hold us to account. You love to pardon, so pardon us. Wipe from our record the things we are most ashamed of, ease for us what You have commanded, and make our hearts erasers and not ledgers, so that we let go of others as we long for You to let go of us. Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anna.

slides:
Al-Afuw.

The Pardoner.

Most of us know what it is to be forgiven and still carry the thing. The other person let it go, and yet it stays there, a small permanent mark on the record.

This name answers that.

Al-Ghaffar covers the sin. Its root means to veil, to conceal.

Al-Afuw goes further.

The root of afw means to **efface**, to wipe away until no trace is left. He does not only hide the record. He erases it.

On Surah Al-Hajj 22:60, al-Sa'di says of this name:

Allah pardons the sinners, forgives their sins, **removes them, and removes their very traces from them.**

Not a stain covered over. A stain washed out completely.

And He does it while fully able to punish.

'...indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and **Competent** [fully Able].' (4:149)

Ibn Kathir: among His attributes is that He pardons although He is fully able to punish. The power is what makes it mercy.

Look where this name sits in 4:43.

A ruling about prayer ends: when there is no water, purify with clean earth.

Ibn Kathir: part of His pardoning is that He made this easy for you. His afw even eases the small hardships.

Of all the prayers for the greatest night of the year, the Prophet ﷺ taught Aisha one, built entirely on this name:

**Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anni.**

O Allah, You are Pardoning, You love to pardon, so pardon me. (at-Tirmidhi)

Sit with the middle of it.

Not 'forgive me' first, but: **You love to pardon.**

Erasing your sins is something He delights in, not something you have to wear Him down to get. 🤍

Because He erases, He tells you to erase.

Al-Sa'di: pardon and overlook, so that Allah deals with you as you deal with His servants.

Hold others to their worst moment, and you ask to be held to yours.

You do not have to live marked by your worst day.

To Al-Afuw, the sincere returner does not carry a covered-over stain. The page is wiped, as though it never was.

Ya Afuw. Erase what we are most ashamed of.
social.caption:
Most of us know what it is to be forgiven and still carry the thing. Forgiveness covered it. It did not erase it.

Al-Afuw, the Pardoner, answers that. Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Qurtubi), this name means more than forgiveness. The root of afw is effacement: to wipe away until no trace is left. So where Al-Ghaffar covers the sin, Al-Afuw removes it. On 22:60, al-Sa'di says Allah pardons sinners, forgives their sins, removes them, and removes their very traces from them, and calls this His settled, essential way with His servants.

And He does it while fully able to punish. In 4:149 the name is paired with qadir, 'fully Able.' Ibn Kathir notes Allah pardons although He is perfectly able to punish, which is exactly what makes it mercy and not mere resignation.

This is the name the Prophet ﷺ pointed his ummah toward on the greatest night of the year. He taught Aisha to say on Laylat al-Qadr: Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anni, 'O Allah, You are Pardoning, You love to pardon, so pardon me' (reported by at-Tirmidhi). Of all requests, He built it on this one name, and on the truth that He loves to erase our sins.

And because He erases, He commands you to. Al-Sa'di: pardon others, so that Allah deals with you as you deal with His servants.

What is the thing you are most ashamed of? To Al-Afuw, the sincere returner does not carry a covered-over stain. The page is wiped. 🤍

email.subject: The name that does not just forgive, it erases
email.preview: Al-Afuw, the Pardoner. The difference between a sin covered over and a sin wiped clean, and the dua the Prophet ﷺ built Laylat al-Qadr around.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Most of us know what it is to be forgiven and still carry the thing. The other person says it is fine, they have let it go, and yet the memory of it sits there between you, a small permanent mark on the record. Forgiveness covered it. It did not erase it. And quietly we assume that is the best even God offers: that our worst moments are pardoned but never quite gone, still technically on the books.

One of the names of Allah answers that assumption directly: Al-Afuw, the Pardoner.

This name does not only mean the One who forgives. The scholars of the language tell us that afw means effacement, the wiping away of a thing until no trace of it is left. So where the names Al-Ghaffar and Al-Ghafur cover the sin (their root means to veil, to conceal), Al-Afuw goes further and removes it. He does not only hide the record, He erases it, as though it had never been written. Commenting on Surah Al-Hajj 22:60, the scholar al-Sa'di says that Allah pardons the sinners and forgives their sins, and then the key phrase: He removes them, and He removes their very traces from them. Not a stain covered over with the scar still showing, but a stain washed out completely.

And He does this while fully able to do otherwise. In Surah An-Nisa the name is paired with power: 'indeed, Allah is ever Pardoning and Competent' (4:149). Ibn Kathir explains that among Allah's attributes is that He pardons His servants although He is fully able to punish them. This is no helpless overlooking. He erases what He had every right and every power to hold against you, and that is exactly what makes it mercy.

If you want to feel how central this name is, look at what the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, taught Aisha to say on the greatest night of the year. She asked him what to say if she knew it was Laylat al-Qadr, the Night of Decree. Of all the things a person could beg for on a night better than a thousand months, he gave her one short prayer, built entirely on this name: 'Allahumma innaka afuwwun tuhibbu al-afwa faʿfu anni,' O Allah, You are Pardoning, You love to pardon, so pardon me (reported by at-Tirmidhi). Notice it does not lead with a list of requests. It approaches Allah by this name, then says something astonishing about Him, that He loves to pardon, that erasing our sins is something He delights in, and only then asks: so erase mine.

And because He erases, He asks you to. Al-Sa'di, on that same verse in Al-Hajj, turns to the one who has been wronged and says: pardon and overlook and forgive, so that Allah will deal with you as you deal with His servants. The standard you set is the standard you invite.

So here is the invitation of this name. You do not have to live as someone permanently marked by your worst day. You do not approach Al-Afuw by first cleaning yourself up enough to deserve pardon. You approach Him because He loves to pardon. Bring Him the thing you are most ashamed of, and ask the One who delights in erasing to erase it.

Ya Afuw, You love to pardon, so pardon us, and wipe from our record the things we would give anything to undo.
